Small Modular Reactors: Still too expensive, too slow and too ...
IEEFA Report Finds That Small Modular Reactors are Still Too ...

findings: SMR proponents claim new designs will avoid the cost overruns and schedule delays of large reactors, but the few SMRs built or started show a pattern strikingly similar to the past, with significant construction delays still the norm and costs continuing to climb.

IEEFA's analysis of the four SMRs currently in operation or under construction, plus projected cost data from leading U.S. SMR developers, shows SMRs are still too expensive, too slow to build, and too risky, with little change from previous assessments.
On-the-ground experience with initial SMRs that have been built or are under construction shows they are not economically competitive, despite proponents claiming otherwise.

Experience with operating and proposed SMRs shows that reactors continue to cost far more and take much longer to build than promised by proponents, with cost overruns and schedule delays remaining the industry norm.

The NuScale SMR project in Idaho saw its costs nearly double from initial projections, ultimately leading to its cancellation, illustrating how SMR cost and schedule estimates can be severely understated.
